documents.filename: High throughput method for simultaneous screening of membrane permeability and toxicity for discovery of new cryoprotective agents.pdf
documents.pdf_path: papers/High throughput method for simultaneous screening of membrane permeability and toxicity for discovery of new cryoprotective agents.pdf
documents.title: High throughput method for simultaneous screening of membrane permeability and toxicity for discovery of new cryoprotective agents
documents.citation: Ahmadkhani N, Benson JD, Eroglu A, Higgins AZ. Scientific Reports. 2025;15:1862. doi:10.1038/s41598-025-85509-x.
documents.abstract: Introduces a 96-well fluorescence workflow that measures membrane permeability and short-term toxicity in the same plate for 27 candidate cryoprotective agents at 4 C and 25 C. The method is about 100 times faster than prior permeability workflows and identified 23 of 27 compounds as promising on the combined permeability and toxicity screen.
documents.notes: Best source for first-pass molecule universe and permeability-oriented replacement features.

documents.filename: High-Throughput Evaluation of Cryoprotective Agents for.pdf
documents.pdf_path: papers/High-Throughput Evaluation of Cryoprotective Agents for.pdf
documents.title: High-Throughput Evaluation of Cryoprotective Agents for Mixture Effects That Reduce Toxicity
documents.citation: Ahmadkhani N, Sugden C, Benson JD, Eroglu A, Higgins AZ. bioRxiv. 2025. doi:10.1101/2025.05.02.651925.
documents.abstract: Uses automated liquid handling to test 21 compounds at higher concentration and 46 binary mixtures of 11 selected compounds on bovine pulmonary artery endothelial cells at room temperature. The paper demonstrates that toxicity rises with concentration and exposure duration, while several binary mixtures significantly reduce toxicity relative to their single-component controls.
documents.notes: Best source for room-temperature concentration dependence and early mixture-rescue signals.

documents.filename: Screening for cryoprotective agent toxicity and toxicity reduction in mixtures at subambient temperatures.pdf
documents.pdf_path: papers/Screening for cryoprotective agent toxicity and toxicity reduction in mixtures at subambient temperatures.pdf
documents.title: Screening for cryoprotective agent toxicity and toxicity reduction in mixtures at subambient temperatures
documents.citation: Ahmadkhani N, Sugden C, Mayo AT, Higgins AZ. bioRxiv. 2025. doi:10.1101/2025.05.07.652719.
documents.abstract: Extends the high-throughput CPA toxicity workflow to 4 C using a cooled liquid-handling platform. The study tests 22 individual CPAs and 169 binary-mixture conditions across 6 and 12 mol/kg, shows strong temperature dependence of toxicity, and finds multiple examples of toxicity reduction and toxicity neutralization at subambient temperature.
documents.notes: Most important source for practical cryo design because it tests the same mixture logic at the 4 C equilibration temperature used in organ and tissue workflows.

documents.filename: Validation of a high-throughput screening assay for the characterization of cryoprotective agent.pdf
documents.pdf_path: papers/Validation of a high-throughput screening assay for the characterization of cryoprotective agent.pdf
documents.title: Validation of a high-throughput screening assay for the characterization of cryoprotective agent toxicity
documents.citation: Jaskiewicz JJ, Callahan-Muller A, Gaby-Biegel N, Glover Z, Sandlin RD. bioRxiv. 2025. doi:10.1101/2025.05.26.654916.
documents.abstract: Develops and validates a T24-cell high-throughput toxicity assay for CPA cocktails using standard HTS quality metrics. The pilot screen covers 587 cocktails from 3.5 M to 8 M and identifies 5 M as the most informative concentration for future large-scale discovery work.
documents.notes: Best source for discovery-pipeline design and for selecting the right concentration band for future cocktail screens.

cpa_structures.name: VS55
cpa_structures.notes: Curated benchmark seed for hypothesis generation. Workhorse vitrification cocktail with strong glass-forming performance and a notable formamide toxicity burden.

cpa_structures.name: DP6
cpa_structures.notes: Curated benchmark seed representing a lower-complexity, lower-toxicity reference point than VS55, but with harder cooling and warming requirements.

cpa_structures.name: M22
cpa_structures.notes: Curated benchmark seed for organ-scale vitrification. Included as a canonical discovery anchor even though its composition does not come from the four screening papers.

cpa_structures.name: FA_GLY_12M_4C
cpa_structures.notes: Assay-defined mixture seeded from the 4 C Ahmadkhani paper because it is the clearest toxicity-neutralization example in the current evidence set.

replacement_entries.name: dimethyl sulfoxide
replacement_entries.notes: Core penetrating CPA with strong permeability and repeated evidence as a rescue partner for formamide and acetamide at 4 C.

replacement_entries.name: ethylene glycol
replacement_entries.notes: Lowest-toxicity member of the seven-molecule HTS panel and the only 6 mol/kg room-temperature single agent that retained high viability in the BPAEC screen.

replacement_entries.name: 1,2-propanediol
replacement_entries.notes: Fast-permeating glycol that becomes markedly safer at 4 C than at room temperature.

replacement_entries.name: formamide
replacement_entries.notes: Very fast-permeating amide with strong toxicity burden that is repeatedly rescued by glycerol and DMSO in mixture experiments.

replacement_entries.name: glycerol
replacement_entries.notes: Slowest of the common penetrating CPAs in the first paper but a major toxicity-rescue partner, especially with formamide.

replacement_entries.name: diethylene glycol
replacement_entries.notes: Follow-on candidate that formed significant toxicity-reducing room-temperature pairs with both 1,2-propanediol and 1,3-propanediol.

replacement_entries.name: 1,3-propanediol
replacement_entries.notes: Useful bridge compound between the BPAEC mixture papers and the later seven-molecule HTS panel.

replacement_entries.name: acetamide
replacement_entries.notes: Second amide of interest because 4 C data suggests its toxicity can also be neutralized by DMSO, glycerol, 2-methoxyethanol, and ethylene glycol.

replacement_entries.name: n-methylacetamide
replacement_entries.notes: Amide-like candidate that stayed in the room-temperature and 4 C follow-on screens but had weaker rescue evidence than formamide or acetamide.

replacement_entries.name: dimethylacetamide
replacement_entries.notes: Extremely fast-permeating candidate in the first screen that also advanced to the 4 C mixture study.

replacement_entries.name: 2-methoxyethanol
replacement_entries.notes: Fast-permeating candidate that repeatedly appears as an amide rescue partner in the 4 C paper.

replacement_entries.name: 1,3-dihydroxyacetone
replacement_entries.notes: Moderate-permeability candidate that remained in the higher-concentration 4 C study.

replacement_entries.name: 2,3-butanediol
replacement_entries.notes: Fast-to-high permeability polyol that survived the first paper and stayed in both mixture follow-on studies.

replacement_entries.name: 2-methyl-1,3-propanediol
replacement_entries.notes: Advanced candidate that remained viable enough to enter both the room-temperature and 4 C higher-concentration screens.

replacement_entries.name: 2-methyl-2,4-pentanediol
replacement_entries.notes: Passed the initial low-concentration screen but became clearly toxic in the 4 C 3 mol/kg follow-up.

replacement_entries.name: diglyme
replacement_entries.notes: One of the compounds that looked acceptable in the initial screen but failed the 4 C 3 mol/kg higher-concentration follow-up.

replacement_entries.name: triglyme
replacement_entries.notes: Another glyme-ether candidate that degraded sharply in the 4 C 3 mol/kg follow-up assay.

replacement_entries.name: tetraethylene glycol dimethyl ether
replacement_entries.notes: Rapidly permeating ether candidate that failed the 4 C 3 mol/kg follow-up.

replacement_entries.name: tetrahydrofurfuryl alcohol
replacement_entries.notes: Fast candidate that did not translate into acceptable 4 C 3 mol/kg viability.

replacement_entries.name: triethylene glycol diacetate
replacement_entries.notes: Illustrates why the later 4 C and 25 C context matters more than the first-pass 4 C permeability signal alone.

replacement_entries.name: triethylene glycol
replacement_entries.notes: Moderate-permeability glycol from the initial screen that did not appear in the later higher-concentration mixture panels.

replacement_entries.name: propionamide
replacement_entries.notes: High-permeability amide that became clearly toxic in the 4 C 3 mol/kg follow-up.

replacement_entries.name: triethanolamine
replacement_entries.notes: Important negative example because amine-containing compounds became toxic despite otherwise interesting chemistry.

replacement_entries.name: pyridine
replacement_entries.notes: Clear toxic failure in the initial dual-temperature screen.

replacement_entries.name: ethanolamine
replacement_entries.notes: Toxic failure that supports a broad caution around ethanolamine-like compounds.

replacement_entries.name: n,n-dimethylethanolamine
replacement_entries.notes: Toxic failure in both temperature conditions of the initial screen.

replacement_entries.name: diethylene glycol monobutyl ether
replacement_entries.notes: One of the four toxic failures in the 4 C initial screen.

replacement_entries.name: 3-methoxy-1,2-propanediol
replacement_entries.notes: Included in the seven-molecule T24 HTS panel where it ranked more toxic than glycerol and 1,3-propanediol but less toxic than urea and DMSO.

replacement_entries.name: urea
replacement_entries.notes: Most toxic compound in the seven-molecule T24 HTS ranking and overrepresented among poor-performing 5 M cocktails.

findings.finding_summary: The 96-well fluorescence workflow measured permeability for about 100 samples in around 30 minutes while also estimating toxicity in the same plate.
Twenty-three of the twenty-seven screened chemicals were retained as promising based on the combined permeability and toxicity criteria.
At 4 C the toxic failures were N,N-dimethylethanolamine, ethanolamine, diethylene glycol monobutyl ether, and pyridine.
At 25 C the toxic failures expanded to six chemicals, adding triethanolamine and triethylene glycol diacetate to the four failures already seen at 4 C.
Dimethyl sulfoxide had measured CPA permeability of 20.62 x 10^-3 s^-1 at 4 C in the BPAEC assay.
Dimethyl sulfoxide had measured CPA permeability of 347.86 x 10^-3 s^-1 at 25 C in the BPAEC assay.
Ethylene glycol had measured CPA permeability of 15.73 x 10^-3 s^-1 at 4 C.
Ethylene glycol had measured CPA permeability of 317.07 x 10^-3 s^-1 at 25 C.
1,2-propanediol had measured CPA permeability of 26.43 x 10^-3 s^-1 at 4 C.
1,2-propanediol had measured CPA permeability of 527.68 x 10^-3 s^-1 at 25 C.
Formamide had measured CPA permeability of 57.36 x 10^-3 s^-1 at 4 C, making it one of the fastest measured common penetrating CPAs in the screen.
Formamide had measured CPA permeability of 622.51 x 10^-3 s^-1 at 25 C, the highest value among the common penetrating CPAs reported in the table.
Glycerol had measured CPA permeability of 2.36 x 10^-3 s^-1 at 4 C, the slowest of the common penetrating CPAs highlighted by the authors.
Glycerol had measured CPA permeability of 56.77 x 10^-3 s^-1 at 25 C, still much slower than the common glycols and amides.

At 3 mol/kg for 30 minutes at room temperature, only seven single agents retained viability above 80 percent: glycerol, DMSO, 1,2-propanediol, ethylene glycol, diethylene glycol, 1,3-propanediol, and 2-methoxyethanol.
At 6 mol/kg for 30 minutes at room temperature, ethylene glycol was the only single-agent CPA that maintained high viability.
Twenty of forty-six room-temperature binary mixtures outperformed both constituent single agents at the same total concentration, and eight of those cases were statistically significant.
Formamide plus glycerol was one of the four named room-temperature binary pairs that significantly reduced toxicity relative to both 6 mol/kg single-agent controls.
Dimethyl sulfoxide plus 1,3-propanediol was one of the significant room-temperature binary toxicity-reduction pairs.
1,2-propanediol plus diethylene glycol was one of the significant room-temperature binary toxicity-reduction pairs.
1,3-propanediol plus diethylene glycol was one of the significant room-temperature binary toxicity-reduction pairs.
The 4 C study evaluated 22 individual CPAs and 219 total compositions using a cooled automated liquid-handling workflow.

At 4 C and 3 mol/kg, diglyme, triglyme, 2-methyl-2,4-pentanediol, tetraethylene glycol dimethyl ether, triethylene glycol diacetate, tetrahydrofurfuryl alcohol, and propionamide were significantly toxic at both 10 and 30 minute exposures.
At 4 C, 42 percent of the 6 mol/kg single-agent and binary-mixture compositions yielded viability above 80 percent, whereas 12 mol/kg conditions were much more toxic.
Forty-three of the fifty-four matched compositions showed significantly higher viability at 4 C than at room temperature.
1,2-propanediol was fully toxic at room temperature but only mildly toxic at 4 C in the matched 6 mol/kg comparison.
At 4 C and 6 mol/kg, 47 of 87 binary mixtures outperformed both matching single-agent controls, and 12 of those cases were statistically significant.
At 4 C and 12 mol/kg, 17 of 50 analyzable binary mixtures outperformed both single-agent controls, and 8 of those cases were statistically significant.

conditions_json.comparison_basis: mixture compared to single components at half total concentration

findings.finding_summary: The 4 C study found 18 statistically significant neutralization cases, but only 9 had viability high enough to be considered meaningfully useful.

At 4 C, the 12 mol/kg formamide plus glycerol mixture yielded 97 percent viability, compared with 20 percent viability for 6 mol/kg formamide alone, making it the strongest neutralization example in the paper.
Dimethyl sulfoxide neutralized the toxicity of both formamide and acetamide in the 4 C mixture study.
The 4 C study concluded that the amides formamide and acetamide each showed rescue interactions with four partners: glycerol, DMSO, 2-methoxyethanol, and ethylene glycol.
The T24-based HTS assay achieved a Z-factor of 0.75 with acceptable drift, supporting its use as a primary cocktail-discovery screen.
The pilot HTS campaign screened 587 unique cocktails over 3.5 to 8 M, completed 2,352 experiments, and achieved a 95 percent plate success rate.
The validation paper identifies 5 M as the most informative primary-screen concentration because it gave a wide viability range with a manageable 1.7 percent hit rate.
In the seven-molecule T24 panel, toxicity ranked from highest to lowest as urea, DMSO, 1,2-propanediol, 3-methoxy-1,2-propanediol, glycerol, 1,3-propanediol, and ethylene glycol.

In the 5 M pilot-screen subset, cocktails that beat toxicity expectations were often enriched for glycerol, DMSO, ethylene glycol, and 1,2-propanediol, while unexpectedly toxic cocktails more often contained urea.
